metadata.title: Why AI Makes Assessment Cheat-Proof or Irrelevant — There Is No Middle Ground

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The idea that detection responds to the absence of evidence rather than to student thinking reframed the whole ban-versus-welcome trap for me. I teach a take-home policy-analysis essay that Claude could draft in a minute. Instead of adding 'no AI,' I want to redesign it like the sociology professor. Can you: (1) name where in my current prompt AI could satisfy the requirement without student thinking, (2) design a sequenced artifact trail with an early annotated bibliography and a late-introduced novel case, and (3) tell me what each added piece actually proves that the final essay alone cannot?

props.command: The idea that detection responds to the absence of evidence rather than to student thinking reframed the whole ban-versus-welcome trap for me. I teach a take-home policy-analysis essay that Claude could draft in a minute. Instead of adding 'no AI,' I want to redesign it like the sociology professor. Can you: (1) name where in my current prompt AI could satisfy the requirement without student thinking, (2) design a sequenced artifact trail with an early annotated bibliography and a late-introduced novel case, and (3) tell me what each added piece actually proves that the final essay alone cannot?
props.segment: Why AI Makes Assessment Cheat-Proof or

beats.narration_text: A take-home essay that bans AI and a take-home essay that welcomes it can both fail to measure student learning — for exactly the same structural reason.

card.copy: Why AI makes assessment cheat-proof or irrelevant

beats.narration_text: A sociology professor assigns a five-page take-home essay. Claude can produce a structurally coherent version in ninety seconds. She adds 'no AI' to the rubric. A student submits Claude's essay lightly edited. A detection tool flags a different student — an English-language learner writing in her authentic voice.
beats.scene_description: A university professor at her desk reviewing a stack of printed student essays, concerned expression, red pen in hand. One essay is highlighted differently. Editorial newsprint documentary style.
beats.image_prompt: B02, university professor at a desk reviewing a tall stack of printed student essays, concerned expression, red pen in hand, one essay on top highlighted with a paper clip, warm desk lamp light, editorial documentary desaturated style, flat newsprint tone, no digital devices visible

beats.narration_text: An AI prohibition should protect assessment validity. Here is the case where it produced a false accusation and let the actual substitution through. Why?

card.copy: An AI ban should protect validity. It flagged the wrong student. Why?

beats.narration_text: Here is how most professors think about AI and academic integrity. Ban the tool. Add a detection layer. Students who follow the rules get through; students who cheat get caught.

production_viz.label: naive model: ban + detect -> integrity
production_viz.mechanic: Three TEAL blocks in a chain: 'AI BAN' -> 'DETECTION LAYER' -> 'VALID ASSESSMENT'. Appears in sequence.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] naive chain: AI ban -> detection -> valid assessment

beats.narration_text: But detection responds to the absence of evidence — it looks for AI signals, not for student thinking. An assignment that can be completed by AI without the student's thinking is a design failure. Banning the tool does not redesign the assignment.

production_viz.label: detection vs. design failure
production_viz.mechanic: The B04 chain returns. Between 'DETECTION LAYER' and 'VALID ASSESSMENT', a CRIMSON gap opens. A CRIMSON label drops in: 'design never changed'. Below: 'detection responds to absence of evidence'.
production_viz.note: the mechanism: the problem is the assignment design, not the tool

beats.narration_text: The professor adds three changes to the sociology essay. One: an annotated bibliography due one week early — students' own words on each source. Two: a novel case introduced one week before the deadline. Three: a five-minute office-hour check-in.

card.copy: Three design moves produce the evidence

beats.narration_text: Move one — the annotated bibliography, due a week early, in the student's own words. This creates a sequenced artifact trail. A student who has not read cannot annotate. And it must arrive before the final draft — so the reading must happen first.

production_viz.label: three design moves as a timeline
production_viz.mechanic: A horizontal timeline appears. Three TEAL milestone markers appear in sequence at Week 1 ('Annotated bibliography — own words'), Week 2 ('Novel case introduced'), and Week 3 ('Office-hour check-in'). Each marker has a brief serif label.
production_viz.note: the example from the chapter's worked redesign

beats.narration_text: Move two — the novel case introduced one week before the deadline. Claude cannot have been primed on a case the student has not seen yet. A transfer task draws on what the student has actually learned.

production_viz.label: the transfer task — late-introduced case
production_viz.mechanic: Two column cards side by side. Left (CRIMSON border): 'General topic — AI can be pre-primed'. Right (TEAL border): 'Novel case, introduced late — draws on internalized learning'. A serif label: 'transfer measures what was actually learned'.
production_viz.note: the contrast that makes transfer work

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] general topic vs. novel case — contrast

beats.narration_text: Move three — the five-minute check-in. Tell me about the source you found most useful and what you took from it. A student who read and engaged can answer. A student who submitted without reading cannot. That conversation is assessment doing what assessment is supposed to do.

production_viz.label: the oral check — two outcomes
production_viz.mechanic: A question card: 'Tell me about your most useful source.' Two student outcome paths fork below it. Left (TEAL): engaged student path — 'Can answer, explains reasoning'. Right (CRIMSON): substitution path — 'Cannot explain, difficulty visible'. A serif label: 'this is evidence'.
production_viz.note: the fork — engagement visible vs. substitution visible

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] oral check fork — engaged vs. substituted

beats.narration_text: The revised design is not foolproof. But it creates multiple points of evidence, not one. AI substitution becomes visible in at least two of three components. And it rewards students who did the learning work.

card.copy: Redesign produces evidence. Detection responds to the absence of it.
card.attribution: — the distinction that changes the design problem

beats.narration_text: The practice: for your next high-stakes take-home, ask one diagnostic question — what can Claude produce that would pass this assignment without the student understanding the material? If the honest answer is 'most of it,' add one process component, one transfer element, one check. Not all three. Pick the highest-leverage point.

production_viz.label: the one diagnostic question
production_viz.mechanic: A notepad card. Text writes in: 'What can Claude produce that would pass this assignment without the student understanding the material?' Below it, a TEAL bracket labeled 'the diagnostic'. Then a serif instruction: 'if the answer is most of it — add one design move'.
production_viz.note: the concrete practice — one question, one move

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the diagnostic question written on a notepad

beats.narration_text: The goal is not to catch cheating. It is to make cheating pointless. An assignment that produces its own evidence of student thinking does not need detection. The design is the integrity.

production_viz.label: repaired chain: design -> evidence -> validity
production_viz.mechanic: Three TEAL blocks in a chain: 'OUTCOME-GROUNDED DESIGN' -> 'MULTIPLE EVIDENCE POINTS' -> 'VALID ASSESSMENT'. All connections solid TEAL. A serif label: 'the design is the integrity'.
production_viz.note: the repaired model — no detection needed

beats.narration_text: No middle ground: the assignment produces evidence of student thinking — or it doesn't. The design is the choice.

card.copy: Detection responds to absence. Design produces evidence.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The goal is not to catch cheating. It is to make cheating pointless. An assignment that produces its own evidence of student thinking does not need detection. The design is the integrity. No middle ground: the assignment produces evidence of student thinking — or it doesn't. The design is the choice.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why AI Makes Assessment Cheat-Proof or Irrelevant — There Is No Middle] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
